item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it, but it again makes me long for a way to compare rewrites like this to the pre-rewrite version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Archon means that containing it would lead to severe endangerment of humanity

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ugh, which one was it? i only remember like 5 unconventional object classes…

apollyon: uncontainable, imminent threat

maksur: must be separated into several pieces to prevent an Apollyon class entity

Thamuiel: helps contain entities

Euclid-Impetus: Uncontainable, but otherwise harmless. if contained, will actively attempt to break containment.

uh… and I think Archon is "uncontainable because the cost of containing it outweighs the benefits." or something. i dunno

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Digging the rewrite. Perfect example of how to handle an extant cryptid myth. There is plenty of creative liberty taken with this one while maintaining the original concept of the beasts, and I love it.

Not the biggest fan of non-standard object classes though. I think this would work better as a Euclid, as it kind of contains itself in a way, but we don't really understand what is going on to the degree that we could declare them as Safe. I don't think Thaumiel works unless Mr. Eye Monster also gets a designation (which would ruin his surprise appearance later on) or is an SCP himself, and I don't see either happening. Perhaps Archon class refers to SCPs that contain themselves, but I don't know that, so a standard object class would definitely feel better to me. It's nowhere near enough for me to downvote, but I just thought I would point it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is anyone else reminded of meltan? (the pokemon)

also, +1

also, wouldn't thaumiel make more sense for the class? You literally say:

SCP-1661 is thus formally considered the primary containment strategy for this entity.

And the entity is clearly anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Memetic content detected and blocked. Please wait for security personnel to escort you to safety.

Override workstation lockdown.
Override the override.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. I especially love the idea of the Twilight Zone episode being a misinformation tactic. However, what's with the "Archon" classification?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lol yes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I +1'd this awhile back, because I do like this SCP a lot. There are two changes you should make, to keep the SCP page's quality up. One minor change I'd suggest making is designating the mysterious entity that 1661 keeps in check as 1661-2.

Individual instances are highly specialized for a variety of purposes relating to the seizure and deconstruction of airborne crafts such as planes, drones, helicopters, and un-manned spacecraft. Laser-cutters, multi-fingered appendages, and primitive cloaking devices have all been observed.

This line confused me, it seems to start off with describing the types of aircraft they target and then I think it's listing the types of tools they deploy to accomplish these tasks? Should probably rework this line. To describe the aircraft they target and then the tools they deploy as a separate sentence.

I'd also recommend the Hiemal classification as an option, but it's not that widely used and Euclid fits just as well. Maybe you could add to the article with the proposed rewrite. So it includes the original document and updated document? Containment procedures could involve flying unmanned drones over the nesting site and redirecting air traffic to keep the public unaware and to allow SCP-1661 the ability to hunt down their aerial prey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The footnote doesn't display because I used some coding from SCP-2316…

…and don't worry about the Site-5 bit. That place doesn't exist anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but what's with the thing about Site-5? And why doesn't the footnote display at the bottom of the page, like a normal footnote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whew. Now I can sleep tonight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feex'd. Thanks for the heads up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, that final addendum really makes this.

That said, just about every instance of the word 'specimen' in this article needs to be 'specimens'. It's not a plural word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's. Something… Onthewing.

Ok, enough of that. I love it. The fact that it can distinguish between planes that have people in them and ones that don't, and them actually changing their behavior predicated on the presence or absence of humans is a very well thought out and executed effect. Also, the thing that's coming up out of the ground gives just enough detail to make it uncomfortable without overexposing it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, but I miss the old picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gets a narrow upvote from me, though I admit I wish it was more evocative of irl Gremlin myths originating from ww2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a fan of this direction for the article. The formatting on the visual report seems odd though, with the space between the header and the box, as well as the lack of padding inside the box itself. Might just be my own weird preferences though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having these creatures be breaking down vehicles for a purpose greater than (whatever gremlins aim to do by breaking vehicles down) was a cool direction to take the article.

+1

item.title: Gremlins! Gremlins? Gremlins.
item.content:encoded:
This is a rewrite of Adam Henderson's previous file. It was hotly contested, hovering between +5 and -14 in 5 years with just shy of 400 votes total.

I took a small detail from the initial concept (their eyes) and ran with it. Hopefully this does the original proud.

Photo credits:

The image is a combination of this CC0 photo here, and this gearpunk artifact image here, also CC0.

Original article can be found below, for reference:

+ show block
– hide block

Item #: SCP-1661

Object Class: Euclid

Special Containment Procedures: All SCP-1661 specimens are to be contained within a four (4) square meter chamber. Entry is to be made into said chamber by means of an airlock to prevent escape attempts. SCP-1661 specimens are only to be removed from chamber for authorized testing. Mobile Task Force Delta 15 (aka "Red Barons") is permanently assigned to the capture or destruction of wild instances of SCP-1661.

Description: SCP-1661 consists of a swarm of self-replicating automata composed of aircraft components, apparently assembled in a nonfunctional manner. Size, weight and exact appearance differ widely between specimens although a single "eye," multiple propellers and an array of miniaturized industrial tools including equipment for welding and cutting metal. The exact "toolkit" varies depending on the specimen; see attached Document ██-1661 for a complete list. It is notable that attempts by Foundation scientists to assemble common aircraft parts identically to the designs of SCP-1661 specimens universally fail to operate.

SCP-1661 reproduce by means of attacking airplanes in large swarms and removing components of the affected planes, which they then assemble into further SCP-1661 specimens. Attacking swarms of SCP-1661 will primarily remove non-essential parts, allowing for affected airplanes to remain airborne longer for further dismantling. SCP-1661 has shown no responses to prey items other than airplanes, or other artificial flying devices.

Document ██-1661
List of SCP-1661 tools

Tool Apparent Usage
Small cutting laser above "eye" Welding and softening of metal
Two (2) miniaturized circular saws on short extendable mechanical arm Removal and shaping of metal
Two (2) propellers extending from swiveling engine pods Flight
Two (2) segmented metal arms, ending in two-way pincers Object manipulation and anchoring to planes in flight
Large amounts of segmented metal tubing and wiring, apparently installed randomly Unknown
